cards.back: The rotational orientation of a bent sub or downhole motor relative to either the high side of the hole (gravity tool face) or magnetic north (magnetic tool face).

flashcard_materials.topic_name: Lebesgue measure and integration theorems (Fatou, Monotone/Dominated convergence)
flashcard_materials.title: High-Yield Concepts

cards.front: State the Monotone Convergence Theorem (Lebesgue).
cards.back: If \(\{f_n\}\) is a sequence of non-negative measurable functions such that \(f_n(x) \le f_{n+1}(x)\) almost everywhere, and \(f_n \to f\) pointwise a.e., then $$\lim_{n \to \infty} \int f_n \, d\mu = \int f \, d\mu$$

cards.front: State Fatou's Lemma.
cards.back: If \(\{f_n\}\) is a sequence of non-negative measurable functions, then $$\int \liminf_{n \to \infty} f_n \, d\mu \le \liminf_{n \to \infty} \int f_n \, d\mu$$

cards.front: State the Lebesgue Dominated Convergence Theorem.
cards.back: If a sequence of measurable functions \(f_n \to f\) pointwise a.e., and there exists a Lebesgue integrable function \(g\) such that \(|f_n(x)| \le g(x)\) a.e. for all \(n\), then \(f\) is integrable and $$\lim_{n \to \infty} \int f_n \, d\mu = \int f \, d\mu$$

cards.front: Define the outer measure \(m^*(A)\) of a set \(A \subset \mathbb{R}\).
cards.back: $$m^*(A) = \inf \left\{ \sum_{k=1}^\infty \ell(I_k) : A \subset \bigcup_{k=1}^\infty I_k, \text{ where } I_k \text{ are open intervals} \right\}$$

cards.front: State Carathéodory's condition for a set \(E\) to be Lebesgue measurable.
cards.back: A set \(E\) is measurable if for every subset \(A \subset \mathbb{R}\): $$m^*(A) = m^*(A \cap E) + m^*(A \cap E^c)$$

cards.front: What is the Lebesgue measure of the Cantor ternary set?
cards.back: The Lebesgue measure of the Cantor set is zero (despite it being uncountably infinite).

cards.front: If \(f\) is a bounded function on a closed interval \([a,b]\), what is the relationship between Riemann integrability and the set of discontinuities of \(f\)?
cards.back: Lebesgue's Criterion: A bounded function \(f\) is Riemann integrable on \([a,b]\) if and only if the set of its discontinuities has Lebesgue measure zero.

cards.front: If \(f\) and \(g\) are measurable functions, is \(f \circ g\) necessarily measurable?
cards.back: No. The composition of two Lebesgue measurable functions is not necessarily Lebesgue measurable (though it is if \(f\) is Borel measurable).

cards.front: What does Egorov's Theorem state?
cards.back: If a sequence of measurable functions \(f_n\) converges pointwise almost everywhere to \(f\) on a set of finite measure, then for every \(\epsilon > 0\), there exists a subset where the convergence is uniform, and the measure of the set's complement is less than \(\epsilon\).

cards.front: What does Lusin's Theorem state?
cards.back: For every measurable function \(f\) on a set of finite measure and every \(\epsilon > 0\), there exists a continuous function \(g\) such that the measure of the set where \(f(x) \neq g(x)\) is less than \(\epsilon\).

flashcard_materials.topic_name: Eigenvalues, eigenvectors, and Cayley-Hamilton Theorem
flashcard_materials.title: High-Yield Concepts

cards.front: State the Cayley-Hamilton Theorem.
cards.back: Every square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring satisfies its own characteristic equation. If \(p(\lambda) = \det(A - \lambda I) = 0\), then \(p(A) = 0\) (the zero matrix).

cards.front: What is the relationship between the algebraic multiplicity (AM) and geometric multiplicity (GM) of an eigenvalue?
cards.back: $$1 \le \text{GM} \le \text{AM}$$

cards.front: State the necessary and sufficient condition for an \(n \times n\) matrix \(A\) to be diagonalizable.
cards.back: Matrix \(A\) is diagonalizable if and only if for every eigenvalue \(\lambda_i\), the geometric multiplicity equals the algebraic multiplicity (i.e., \(A\) has \(n\) linearly independent eigenvectors).

cards.front: If \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of an invertible matrix \(A\), what is the corresponding eigenvalue of \(A^{-1}\)?
cards.back: $$\lambda^{-1}$$ (or \(1/\lambda\))

cards.front: What can be said about the eigenvalues of a real symmetric matrix?
cards.back: All eigenvalues of a real symmetric matrix are real.

cards.front: What can be said about the eigenvectors corresponding to distinct eigenvalues of a real symmetric matrix?
cards.back: They are orthogonal to each other.

cards.front: How do you find the trace of a matrix using its eigenvalues?
cards.back: The trace of a matrix is equal to the sum of its eigenvalues: $$\text{Tr}(A) = \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i$$

cards.front: How do you find the determinant of a matrix using its eigenvalues?
cards.back: The determinant of a matrix is equal to the product of its eigenvalues: $$\det(A) = \prod_{i=1}^n \lambda_i$$

cards.front: If \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue of \(A\), what is an eigenvalue of \(A^k\)?
cards.back: $$\lambda^k$$

cards.front: What is a Nilpotent matrix, and what are its eigenvalues?
cards.back: A square matrix \(N\) is nilpotent if \(N^k = 0\) for some positive integer \(k\). All eigenvalues of a nilpotent matrix are 0.

flashcard_materials.topic_name: Residue theorem, Argument principle, and Conformal mappings
flashcard_materials.title: High-Yield Concepts

cards.front: State Cauchy's Residue Theorem.
cards.back: If \(f(z)\) is analytic inside and on a simple closed contour \(C\), except for a finite number of isolated singularities \(z_k\) inside \(C\), then: $$\oint_C f(z) dz = 2\pi i \sum_{k=1}^n \text{Res}(f, z_k)$$

cards.front: What is the formula to calculate the residue of \(f(z)\) at a simple pole \(z_0\)?
cards.back: $$\text{Res}(f, z_0) = \lim_{z \to z_0} (z - z_0)f(z)$$

cards.front: What is the formula to calculate the residue of \(f(z)\) at a pole of order \(m\) at \(z_0\)?
cards.back: $$\text{Res}(f, z_0) = \frac{1}{(m-1)!} \lim_{z \to z_0} \frac{d^{m-1}}{dz^{m-1}} \left[ (z - z_0)^m f(z) \right]$$

cards.front: State the Argument Principle.
cards.back: If \(f(z)\) is meromorphic inside and on a simple closed contour \(C\), with no zeros or poles on \(C\), then: $$\frac{1}{2\pi i} \oint_C \frac{f'(z)}{f(z)} dz = N - P$$ where \(N\) is the number of zeros and \(P\) is the number of poles inside \(C\) (counted with multiplicity).

cards.front: State Rouché's Theorem.
cards.back: If \(f(z)\) and \(g(z)\) are analytic inside and on a simple closed contour \(C\), and if \(|g(z)| < |f(z)|\) on \(C\), then \(f(z)\) and \(f(z) + g(z)\) have the same number of zeros inside \(C\).

cards.front: Define a Conformal Mapping.
cards.back: A mapping \(w = f(z)\) is conformal at \(z_0\) if it preserves both the magnitude and the sense (direction) of the angle between any two intersecting curves passing through \(z_0\).

cards.front: What is the necessary and sufficient condition for a mapping \(w = f(z)\) to be conformal at a point \(z_0\)?
cards.back: The function \(f(z)\) must be analytic at \(z_0\) and its derivative must be non-zero: $$f'(z_0) \neq 0$$

cards.front: What is a Möbius Transformation (Fractional Linear Transformation)?
cards.back: A mapping of the form $$w = \frac{az + b}{cz + d}$$ where \(ad - bc \neq 0\). It maps circles and lines in the \(z\)-plane to circles and lines in the \(w\)-plane.

cards.front: In a Laurent series expansion \(f(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^\infty a_n (z-z_0)^n\), which coefficient represents the residue of \(f(z)\) at \(z_0\)?
cards.back: The coefficient \(a_{-1}\).

cards.front: What does the Open Mapping Theorem state for complex functions?
cards.back: If \(f\) is a non-constant analytic function on a connected open set (domain) \(D\), then the image \(f(D)\) is also an open set.

flashcard_materials.topic_name: Complex analysis: Cauchy-Riemann, singularities, and residue theorem
flashcard_materials.title: High-Yield Concepts

cards.front: State the Cauchy-Riemann equations in Cartesian coordinates for an analytic function \(f(z) = u(x,y) + iv(x,y)\).
cards.back: $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial v}{\partial x}$$

cards.front: What is the condition for a real-valued function \(u(x,y)\) to be harmonic?
cards.back: It must satisfy Laplace's equation: $$\nabla^2 u = \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} = 0$$

cards.front: State Cauchy's Integral Formula for an analytic function \(f(z)\) inside and on a simple closed contour \(C\).
cards.back: $$f(z_0) = \frac{1}{2\pi i} \oint_C \frac{f(z)}{z - z_0} dz$$

cards.front: How do you calculate the residue of a function \(f(z)\) at a simple pole \(z=a\)?
cards.back: $$\text{Res}(f, a) = \lim_{z \to a} (z - a)f(z)$$

cards.front: What is the formula for the residue at a pole of order \(m\) at \(z=a\)?
cards.back: $$\text{Res}(f, a) = \frac{1}{(m-1)!} \lim_{z \to a} \frac{d^{m-1}}{dz^{m-1}} [(z - a)^m f(z)]$$

cards.front: State the Residue Theorem for a contour integral over a simple closed path \(C\).
cards.back: $$\oint_C f(z) dz = 2\pi i \sum_{k=1}^N \text{Res}(f, a_k)$$

cards.front: What defines an essential singularity of a complex function?
cards.back: A singularity at \(z=a\) is essential if the principal part of its Laurent series expansion around \(a\) has an infinite number of non-zero terms (e.g., \(e^{1/z}\) at \(z=0\)).

cards.front: What is a removable singularity?
cards.back: A singularity at \(z=a\) is removable if \(\lim_{z \to a} f(z)\) exists and is finite. The Laurent series has no negative power terms.

cards.front: What does Liouville's Theorem state?
cards.back: If a function \(f(z)\) is entire (analytic everywhere in the complex plane) and bounded, then \(f(z)\) must be a constant.

cards.front: If \(f(z)\) is analytic inside and on a simple closed contour \(C\), what is the value of \(\oint_C f(z) dz\)?
cards.back: Zero (by Cauchy's Integral Theorem).

flashcard_materials.topic_name: 1D Potentials: Tunneling, particle in a box, and harmonic oscillator
flashcard_materials.title: High-Yield Concepts

cards.front: What are the energy eigenvalues for a particle of mass \(m\) in a 1D infinite square well of width \(L\)?
cards.back: $$E_n = \frac{n^2 \pi^2 \hbar^2}{2 m L^2}$$ where \(n = 1, 2, 3, \dots\)

cards.front: Write the normalized wave function for a particle in a 1D infinite square well of width \(L\) (from \(x=0\) to \(L\)).
cards.back: $$\psi_n(x) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{L}} \sin\left(\frac{n \pi x}{L}\right)$$

cards.front: What are the energy eigenvalues of a 1D quantum harmonic oscillator with angular frequency \(\omega\)?
cards.back: $$E_n = \left(n + \frac{1}{2}\right) \hbar \omega$$ where \(n = 0, 1, 2, \dots\)

cards.front: What is the ground state (zero-point) energy of the 1D quantum harmonic oscillator?
cards.back: $$E_0 = \frac{1}{2} \hbar \omega$$

cards.front: For a 1D harmonic oscillator, what is the action of the raising operator (\(a^\dagger\)) on the energy eigenstate \(|n\rangle\)?
cards.back: $$a^\dagger |n\rangle = \sqrt{n+1} |n+1\rangle$$

cards.front: For a 1D harmonic oscillator, what is the action of the lowering (annihilation) operator (\(a\)) on the energy eigenstate \(|n\rangle\)?
cards.back: $$a |n\rangle = \sqrt{n} |n-1\rangle$$

cards.front: What is the approximate transmission probability \(T\) through a 1D rectangular potential barrier of height \(V_0\) and width \(a\) for a particle of energy \(E < V_0\) (wide/high barrier limit)?
cards.back: $$T \approx e^{-2\kappa a}$$ where $$\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2m(V_0 - E)}}{\hbar}$$

cards.front: What does the Virial Theorem state for a 1D quantum harmonic oscillator?
cards.back: The expectation value of kinetic energy equals the expectation value of potential energy: $$\langle T \rangle = \langle V \rangle = \frac{1}{2} \langle E \rangle$$

cards.front: What is the shape of the ground state wavefunction of a 1D quantum harmonic oscillator?
cards.back: It is a Gaussian function: $$\psi_0(x) \propto e^{-\frac{m\omega}{2\hbar}x^2}$$

cards.front: If the width of a 1D infinite potential well is doubled, how does the ground state energy change?
cards.back: The ground state energy decreases by a factor of 4 (since \(E_1 \propto 1/L^2\)).

flashcard_materials.topic_name: Maxwell's equations, scalar/vector potentials, and gauges
flashcard_materials.title: High-Yield Concepts

cards.front: Write Maxwell's equations in differential form in a vacuum (with charge density \(\rho\) and current density \(\mathbf{J}\)).
cards.back:
1. $$
abla \cdot \mathbf{E} =  rac{ho}{\epsilon_0}$$
2. $$
abla \cdot \mathbf{B} = 0$$
3. $$
abla 	imes \mathbf{E} = - rac{\partial \mathbf{B}}{\partial t}$$
4. $$
abla 	imes \mathbf{B} = \mu_0 \mathbf{J} + \mu_0 \epsilon_0  rac{\partial \mathbf{E}}{\partial t}$$

cards.front: Express the electric field \(\mathbf{E}\) and magnetic field \(\mathbf{B}\) in terms of the scalar potential \(V\) and vector potential \(\mathbf{A}\).
cards.back:
$$\mathbf{E} = -\nabla V - \frac{\partial \mathbf{A}}{\partial t}$$
$$\mathbf{B} = \nabla \times \mathbf{A}$$

cards.front: What is the condition for the Coulomb gauge?
cards.back: $$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{A} = 0$$

cards.front: What is the condition for the Lorenz gauge?
cards.back: $$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{A} + \frac{1}{c^2} \frac{\partial V}{\partial t} = 0$$ (or $$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{A} + \mu_0 \epsilon_0 \frac{\partial V}{\partial t} = 0$$)

cards.front: Write the inhomogeneous wave equation for the scalar potential \(V\) under the Lorenz gauge.
cards.back: $$\Box^2 V = \nabla^2 V - \frac{1}{c^2} \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial t^2} = -\frac{\rho}{\epsilon_0}$$

cards.front: Write the inhomogeneous wave equation for the vector potential \(\mathbf{A}\) under the Lorenz gauge.
cards.back: $$\Box^2 \mathbf{A} = \nabla^2 \mathbf{A} - \frac{1}{c^2} \frac{\partial^2 \mathbf{A}}{\partial t^2} = -\mu_0 \mathbf{J}$$

cards.front: What are gauge transformations for \(V\) and \(\mathbf{A}\) using an arbitrary scalar function \(\lambda(\mathbf{r},t)\)?
cards.back:
$$\mathbf{A}' = \mathbf{A} + \nabla \lambda$$
$$V' = V - \frac{\partial \lambda}{\partial t}$$

cards.front: What is the physical significance of Gauge Invariance?
cards.back: The observable physical fields (\(\mathbf{E}\) and \(\mathbf{B}\)) remain unchanged under a gauge transformation of the potentials (\(V\) and \(\mathbf{A}\)).

cards.front: What is the continuity equation representing the conservation of electric charge?
cards.back: $$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{J} + \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$$

cards.front: Which of Maxwell's equations mathematically demands the introduction of the displacement current?
cards.back: Ampere's Law ($\nabla \times \mathbf{B} = \mu_0 \mathbf{J}$). Without displacement current, taking the divergence of both sides yields $0 = \nabla \cdot \mathbf{J}$, violating the continuity equation for non-steady currents.

exam.name: GATE MN
exam.full_name: Graduate Aptitude Test in Engineering - Mining Engineering
exam.description: National level exam for admission to postgraduate programs in Mining Engineering organized by IIT Guwahati

topics.name: Linear Algebra: Matrices, Determinants, Systems of Equations, and Eigen values/vectors
Calculus: Limits, Partial derivatives, Maxima/minima, and Fourier series
Vector Calculus: Gradient, Divergence, Curl, and Vector theorems
Differential Equations: Linear/nonlinear ODEs and Cauchy's/Euler's equations
Probability and Statistics: Distributions, Hypothesis testing, and Regression
Numerical Methods: Solutions of algebraic equations, Interpolation, and Integration rules

subjects.name: Mining Geology, Mine Development and Surveying

topics.name: Mining Geology: Minerals, Rocks, Ore Genesis, and Structural Geology
Mine Development: Access methods, Underground drivages, Drilling, and Blasting design
Mine Surveying: Levelling, Triangulation, Errors, Curves, and Photogrammetry
Advanced Surveying: Total Station, GPS, GIS, and Remote Sensing basics
Engineering Mechanics: Equivalent force systems, Equilibrium, Trusses, and Rigid body dynamics
Geomechanics: Rock properties, classification, failure theories, and stress measurement
Ground Response: Stress distribution, Subsidence, and Slope stability
Ground Control: Pillar design, Roof supporting systems, and Strata Monitoring
Surface Mining: Layout, Development, Mechanization, and Highwall mining
Underground Coal Mining: Bord and Pillar, Longwall, and Thick seam methods
Underground Metal Mining: Stoping methods, mechanization, and ore handling
Mining Machinery: Power transmission, Materials handling (Conveyors, Hoisting), and Pumps

subjects.name: Surface Environment, Mine Ventilation and Underground Hazards

topics.name: Surface Environment: Air/Water/Soil pollution, Noise control, and EIA
Mine Ventilation: Underground atmosphere, Thermal environment, and Ventilation networks
Underground Hazards: Mine Gases, Fires, Explosions, Dust, and Inundation
Safety and Legislation: Rescue practices, Safety management plan, and Mine legislation

subjects.name: Mineral Economics, Mine Planning, Systems Engineering

topics.name: Mineral Economics: Resource classification, DCF analysis, and Valuation
Mine Planning: Reserve estimation, Geostatistics, and Ultimate pit configuration
Systems Engineering: Reliability, LP, Network analysis, and Queuing theory
